explanation: SET NOCOUNT OFF leaves row-count chatter enabled, increasing client and network noise.
SET XACT_ABORT OFF allows some run-time errors to leave a transaction partially applied.
SELECT * couples callers to every current and future column of Sales.Orders.
SELECT TOP (10) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary set of rows.
DISTINCT is unnecessary because OrderID is already guaranteed unique as the primary key.
WITH (NOLOCK) can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated rows.
Applying YEAR() to the indexed OrderDate column prevents a simple range seek.
Comparing int column CustomerID with nvarchar variable @CustomerIdText forces implicit conversion.
OR-based optional filtering (@CustomerID IS NULL OR ...) makes cardinality estimation and index seeking difficult.
LIKE pattern starting with '%' prevents a normal index seek on Comments.
NOT IN can produce surprising empty results if the subquery returns any NULL values.
Wrapping CustomerID in ISNULL makes the predicate non-sargable, preventing index usage.
Arithmetic operation (OrderID + 0) on the column prevents direct index matching.
Inequality predicate (<>) often results in scanning broad portions of the table.
CROSS JOIN omits a join predicate and multiplies rows.
SELECT TOP (1) lacks an ORDER BY clause, resulting in arbitrary row selection.
Comma-separated tables in FROM with join in WHERE is an old-style join.
SELECT TOP (1) lacks an ORDER BY clause, resulting in arbitrary row selection.
Correlated scalar subquery executes a lookup for every qualifying outer row.
Correlated EXISTS check runs per outer row instead of expressing a set-based join.
The CASE expression is repeated in both the SELECT list and the ORDER BY clause.
ORDER BY 2 refers to a column position, which becomes fragile if the select list changes.
OFFSET FETCH pagination lacks a stable, unique tie-breaker in the ORDER BY clause.
UNION performs needless duplicate removal between branches that are already disjoint.
COUNT(*) > 0 counts all matching rows when only existence (EXISTS) is needed.
SELECT assignment silently accepts multiple rows and keeps the last value without warning.
Table variables may receive poor cardinality estimates, leading to suboptimal query plans.
SELECT TOP (5) lacks an ORDER BY clause, resulting in arbitrary row selection.
SELECT INTO implicitly creates a temp table schema instead of declaring it explicitly.
SELECT TOP (5) lacks an ORDER BY clause, resulting in arbitrary row selection.
Cursor processes rows one-by-one instead of using set-based operations.
SELECT TOP (2) lacks an ORDER BY clause, resulting in arbitrary row selection.
WHILE loop performs iterative row-by-row work instead of a set-based operation.
Concatenating variables directly into executable SQL text is unsafe and prevents plan caching.
EXEC (@Sql) executes dynamic SQL without using parameterized inputs.
Concatenating @OrderID into the SQL string prevents parameterization and plan reuse.
sp_executesql is called without defining and binding parameters.
Temporary table #Scratch is explicitly created but never dropped before the procedure ends.
CATCH block captures the error but neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status.
RAISERROR is used instead of the modern THROW statement for error handling.
PRINT is buffered and unsuitable for structured, real-time diagnostics.
Business identifier '1' is hardcoded instead of being passed as a parameter or looked up.
Unlabelled date constant '20160101' is embedded directly in the query logic.
Date string '01/02/2016' is converted without an explicit style parameter, relying on session settings.
COALESCE in the join predicate inhibits normal join optimization.
SELECT TOP (1) lacks an ORDER BY clause, resulting in arbitrary row selection.
Self-join on Orders retrieves the same key without adding new information.
SELECT TOP (1) lacks an ORDER BY clause, resulting in arbitrary row selection.
Sorting by the wide, unselective text column Comments is highly inefficient.
Aggregating the entire Sales.OrderLines table without a restrictive filter is expensive.
Row-level predicate (OrderID > 0) is placed in HAVING instead of the WHERE clause.
ORDER BY is applied to an aggregate query guaranteed to return only one row.
MERGE statement has documented concurrency, performance, and correctness edge cases.
UPDATE sets Comments to its current value, causing unnecessary logging and locking.
DELETE uses a broad business value (Quantity = -1) rather than a stable primary key.
WAITFOR DELAY intentionally blocks the worker thread instead of using asynchronous scheduling.
BEGIN TRANSACTION is not wrapped in a TRY...CATCH block for proper error handling and rollback.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT ON changes session state and introduces fragile identity-management coupling.
System stored procedure sp_who is invoked without the 'sys.' schema qualifier.
SET ROWCOUNT changes a session setting and is not restored to its original state.
INSERT INTO uses VALUES without an explicit column list, which is fragile to schema changes.
Magic number 99 is hardcoded as a CategoryID instead of using a parameter or lookup.
LIKE pattern starts with a wildcard '%', preventing index seeks on CustomerName.
Concatenating @CustomerName directly into dynamic SQL prevents parameterization and plan reuse.
LIKE pattern starts with a wildcard '%', preventing index seeks on CustomerName.
User-supplied input @CustomerName is concatenated directly into a dynamic SQL string, creating a SQL injection vulnerability.
sp_executesql is executed without defining and binding parameters.
System stored procedure sp_executesql is invoked without the 'sys.' schema qualifier.
UPDATE statement lacks a WHERE clause, modifying all rows in the table and risking unintended data loss.
Hardcoded value '1' is used for CustomerCategoryID instead of a parameter.
Unlabelled date constant '2016-01-01' is embedded directly in the query logic.
Predicate on outer-joined table 'o.OrderDate' in the WHERE clause effectively converts the LEFT JOIN to an INNER JOIN, causing incorrect results.
Using '= NULL' instead of 'IS NULL' will always evaluate to UNKNOWN and incorrectly return no rows.
Scalar UDF dbo.GetCustomerType in the SELECT list forces row-by-row evaluation and inhibits parallelism.
CATCH block captures the error but neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status.
CATCH block fails to roll back the active transaction started in the TRY block, leaving it open and risking data corruption.
Explicit index hint WITH (INDEX = 1) hardcodes physical implementation details into the query.
Query uses excessively nested subqueries (3+ levels) that should be refactored to CTEs or temp tables.
LIKE pattern '%A%' starts with a wildcard, preventing efficient index usage.
SELECT * couples callers to every current and future column of the table-valued function.
Multi-statement TVF dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti is used instead of an inline TVF, causing poor cardinality estimates and performance issues.
